votes.reason: The Python code correctly computes the sum of string lengths for both lists and checks the exact same logical condition as the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly computes the sum of string lengths for both lists and implements the exact same conditional logic as the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly calculates the sum of string lengths for both lists and implements the exact same logical condition as the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly computes the total character counts c1 and c2 using sum(len(s) for s in lst) which is equivalent to Coq's fold_left with string length accumulation. The boolean condition (c1 <= c2 and result == lst1) or (c1 > c2 and result == lst2) directly matches the Coq Prop (c1 <= c2 /\ result = lst1) \/ (c1 > c2 /\ result = lst2). For any concrete inputs, both will return/satisfy the same judgment.
